content.code: Color eyeColor{ 8 }; // syntactically valid, semantically meaningless

content.text: A reminderTo quickly recap on nomenclature:Anenumerationorenumerated typeis the program-defined type itself (e.g.Color).Anenumeratoris a specific named value belonging to the enumeration (e.g.red).
To quickly recap on nomenclature:Anenumerationorenumerated typeis the program-defined type itself (e.g.Color).Anenumeratoris a specific named value belonging to the enumeration (e.g.red).

list: Anenumerationorenumerated typeis the program-defined type itself (e.g.Color).Anenumeratoris a specific named value belonging to the enumeration (e.g.red).
Anenumeratoris a specific named value belonging to the enumeration (e.g.red).

content.text: WarningEnumerations don’t have to be named, but unnamed enumerations should be avoided in modern C++.
Enumerations don’t have to be named, but unnamed enumerations should be avoided in modern C++.
Best practiceName your enumerated types starting with a capital letter. Name your enumerators starting with a lower case letter.
Name your enumerated types starting with a capital letter. Name your enumerators starting with a lower case letter.

For advanced readersClasses also provide a scope region, and it’s common to put enumerated types related to a class inside the scope region of the class. We discuss this in lesson15.3 -- Nested types (member types).
Classes also provide a scope region, and it’s common to put enumerated types related to a class inside the scope region of the class. We discuss this in lesson15.3 -- Nested types (member types).
Best practicePrefer putting your enumerations inside a named scope region (such as a namespace or class) so the enumerators don’t pollute the global namespace.
Prefer putting your enumerations inside a named scope region (such as a namespace or class) so the enumerators don’t pollute the global namespace.

BecauseMonsterTypeis an unscoped enumeration, its enumerators (e.g.troll) are placed into namespace of the enumeration itself (which in this case isnamespace Monster). Thus, we can accesstrollasMonster::troll.Because unscoped enumerations also put their enumerators into their own namespace,trollcan also be accessed asMonster::MonsterType::troll. However, there is no real benefit to doing so.
Because unscoped enumerations also put their enumerators into their own namespace,trollcan also be accessed asMonster::MonsterType::troll. However, there is no real benefit to doing so.
